text: We present to you a video on robotic retroperitoneal adenlectomy for large adrenal lesion, its feasibility and outcome.
Robotic adenlectomy is becoming the treatment option of choice for large adrenal tumors.
However, there is a risk of capsular disruption, incomplete removal and the associated technical difficulties.
The retroperitoneal route provides a direct approach with no bowel handling.
We present to you the perioperative outcomes of robotic adrenalectomy over a period of three years and we present a video of such a case.
A fifty-one year old female patient was found to have a right adrenal mass during evaluation for palpitations.
A CT scan showed a seven point five centimeters enhancing mass in the right adrenal gland.
The functional study was suggestive of a pheochromocytoma
and she was planned for a robotic retroperitoneal adenlectomy after adequate preparation.
The patient is positioned in the left lateral position at the edge of the surgical table, preparing for the robotic adrenalectomy procedure.
The surgeon makes the initial entry point just below the twelfth rib near the mid-axillary line, preparing for the robotic retroperitoneal approach.
The surgeon uses a visiport for direct entry through the fat and muscles, accessing the retroperitoneal space.
Here one has to exert caution not to enter into the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon uses a zero-degree camera in a sweeping motion to expand the retroperitoneal space, providing a clear view of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon inserts the first robotic port into the surgical field, following the initial entry point below the twelfth rib near the mid-axillary line.
The surgeon uses bipolar forceps to create additional space in the retroperitoneal area, facilitating the insertion of two more robotic ports.
The surgeon places a twelve mm assistant port between the first two robotic ports, ensuring proper alignment within the surgical field.
The final port placement is shown, with four robotic ports and one assistant port visibly positioned on the surgical field.
The DaVinci XI robotic system is docked to the surgical ports, preparing for robotic-assisted surgery.
The surgeon performs cranial dissection along the psoas muscle using robotic instruments.
The surgeon identifies the adrenal tumor and performs a circumferential dissection around it using robotic instruments.
The surgeon uses robotic instruments to perform initial dissection, separating the adrenal tumor from the underlying psoas muscle and its fascia.
The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to ligate small feeding vessels supplying the adrenal tumor, ensuring hemostasis during the robotic-assisted procedure.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the adrenal tumor using robotic instruments, minimizing excessive handling to prevent blood pressure fluctuations.
The surgeon creates and further develops a dissection plane between the upper pole of the kidney and the adrenal tumor, carefully separating these structures.
The surgeon dissects the upper part of the adrenal tumor away from the peritoneal lining that overlies the liver, carefully separating the tumor from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon dissects a portion of the tumor that courses beneath the inferior vena cava (IVC), carefully separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon utilizes the retroperitoneal approach to access and safely manage the feeder vessels located behind the inferior vena cava (IVC) that supply the tumor.
The surgeon performs further dissection to identify the main adrenal vein, which is then clipped with wax clips and cut, ensuring careful handling to prevent blood pressure fluctuations.
The surgeon clips the main adrenal vein with wax clips, completing the dissection of the vein. The surgical team communicates with the anesthetist regarding potential hypotension management.
The surgeon releases and cuts the remaining attachments of the tumor using monopolar cautery. The specimen is then placed into an endo bag for removal.
The surgical field reveals the floor of the dissection area, with visible tissue layers and structures exposed following the removal of the tumor and surrounding attachments.
The surgeon observes a portion of the adrenal gland extending behind the inferior vena cava (IVC), highlighting the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The resected right adrenal gland specimen is displayed, showing its size and shape after removal from the surgical site.
We had a total of five cases with the mean age being fifty years.
The average tumor size was six point four six centimeters.
All were right-sided tumors.
Four had a pheochromocytoma and one was a ganglioneuroma.
The mean blood loss was only a thirty mL.
The average stay was two point five days and none of the patients had any intraoperative or postoperative complications.
To conclude, robotic retroperitoneal adrenalectomy is feasible, safe and associated with good outcomes in large adrenal tumors.
